publicationStmt.p: Transcribed from Bodleian Library, Lovelace Byron collection, Box 165, folios 187-189. Transcription by Lisa Gold (2022). TEI conversion for Sextant pipeline, 2026.

div.p: Dearest Mate. I am as careful as you can wish, to burn your letters, but they would be quite safe at any rate, for there is trouble enough to make them out even for me; & I am sure no servant could by any possibility do so. Now don't, dear Old Crow,
fancy that a saucy Thrush is insulting ou, & revenge yourself by not writing. You know how I like to have your letters, & how I admire your hand-writing.
But tho' very pretty, it never is very legible.
In consequence of the enclosed from the Hen, I went myself in the Chariot to meet her; which pleased her, & was more than she expected.

I left her at Lord Byrons & went on to the Royal Institution to a Lecture, sending the carriage back to her for her use this afternoon. So you see the Bird does not neglect that nice Old Gentleman the Hen!--
Today I had my Singing- Lesson at 10 o'clock, for an hour & a half. Then Wheatstone for 2 hours; then to the Railway; then the Lecture. And now I must set to work

to practise my Harp for 4 or 5 hours at least. I have no time for the Theatre tonight, nor have had now scarely for a week. Perhaps tomorrow I may get to one. But you see I attend to my business in real good earnest; despite of there being very special Theatrical attractions just now too.--
Faija is delighted with my progress, & I am
now to begin one or two little Songs; Duetts with him.--
I am well & happy; & I hope doing my duty, & getting on in everything.

It is to Miss Neville that your Uncle is to be married. Hester has had a letter from Lady C. Fellowes; who approves of her Brother's choice in Miss N--; & who also speaks enthusiastically of Lady Fortescue.--
